title: Interfaces and Type Aliases
summary: interface vs type alias, structural typing, declaration merging, and when to reach for each.

questions.prompt: What is structural typing in TypeScript?
choices: Types are matched by name — Dog must be explicitly declared as Dog
Types are matched by shape — if a value has the required fields, it's compatible regardless of its name
Only class instances can satisfy interface types
TypeScript checks inheritance chains at runtime
questions.explanation: TypeScript is structurally typed. A value with `{ x: number; y: number; extra: string }` satisfies `Point = { x: number; y: number }` — the name and extra field don't matter. This is the opposite of nominal typing (Java/C# where the exact class name matters).

questions.prompt: Declaration merging is a feature of:
choices: type aliases only
interface only
both interface and type
neither — you must use classes
questions.explanation: `interface` supports declaration merging: two identically-named interfaces are merged into one. `type` does not — declaring the same type alias name twice is an error.

questions.prompt: Does this compile? Why?
choices: Error — duplicate interface declaration
Compiles — merges both declarations into { host: string; port: number }
Compiles — second interface overrides first
Error — object literal excess property
questions.explanation: Interface declaration merging combines both declarations. The effective shape is `{ host: string; port: number }`. The object satisfies both fields — no error.

questions.prompt: Which of these can ONLY be expressed with a type alias, not an interface?
choices: { name: string; age: number }
string | number
An object that extends another object
A recursive tree node
questions.explanation: `string | number` is a union type — only expressible with `type`. Interfaces cannot represent unions. Object shapes, extension, and recursive types are possible with both.

questions.prompt: Does this produce an error?
choices: Yes — label is not in Point
No — passing via variable bypasses excess property check
Yes — TypeScript always checks all properties
No — because label is a string
questions.explanation: Excess property checks apply only to fresh object literals passed directly. Passing via a variable (`pt`) skips the check — TypeScript only verifies the required fields exist. Pass `draw({ x: 1, y: 2, label: 'origin' })` directly and you'll get an error.

questions.prompt: A class can implement a type alias:
choices: Never — implements only works with interface
Yes — if the type alias describes an object shape (not a union or primitive)
Only if the type alias uses the same name as the class
Yes — but the class must redeclare all fields
questions.explanation: Classes can implement both `interface` and object-shape `type` aliases. `class Foo implements MyType {}` works as long as `MyType` is an object shape, not a union or primitive.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `merged`?
choices: { x: number } | { y: string }
{ x: number; y: string }
{ x: number } or { y: string } depending on value
never — cannot intersect types
questions.explanation: Intersection `A & B` requires a value to satisfy BOTH shapes. The result is `{ x: number; y: string }` — all properties from both types are required.

questions.prompt: What does `readonly` on an interface property enforce?
choices: The property cannot be set in the constructor
The property cannot be reassigned after the object is created — TypeScript compile-time enforcement only
Deep immutability of nested objects
The property is frozen at the JavaScript level
questions.explanation: `readonly` prevents reassignment at the TypeScript compile time level only — the JS output has no protection. It's shallow: `readonly arr: number[]` prevents `arr = []` but NOT `arr.push(1)`. Deep immutability requires `as const` or `ReadonlyArray`.

questions.prompt: Which are valid uses of a type alias (not possible with interface)?
choices: type Status = 'idle' | 'loading' | 'error'
type Pair<T> = [T, T]
type IsArray<T> = T extends any[] ? true : false
type User = { name: string; age: number }
questions.explanation: Union types, tuple types, and conditional types require `type`. Object shapes (option D) can be expressed by both `type` and `interface`.

questions.prompt: A discriminated union works by:
choices: Checking the runtime class of each value
Using a shared literal field ('kind', 'type', 'tag') that TypeScript uses to narrow to the correct member
Requiring all union members to extend a base interface
Comparing value references
questions.explanation: A discriminated (tagged) union has a shared field with a different literal type in each member. TypeScript narrows based on that field — checking `s.kind === 'circle'` tells TS that `s` is the circle variant.

questions.prompt: What does TypeScript infer for `result`?
choices: s is Shape (full union)
s is { kind: 'circle'; radius: number }
s is never
s is { radius: number }
questions.explanation: Inside `if (s.kind === 'circle')`, TypeScript narrows `s` to the circle member of the union. `s.radius` is safe to access because TypeScript knows which variant it is.

questions.prompt: An index signature `[key: string]: unknown` on an interface means:
choices: Only string property names are allowed
Any additional string-keyed properties are allowed, typed as unknown
The interface has no defined properties
TypeScript disables property checking
questions.explanation: An index signature says: 'this object may have any number of string keys, and their values are typed as unknown (or whatever the index value type is)'. Explicitly declared properties must also be assignable to the index value type.

questions.prompt: Why might you choose `interface` over `type` for a public library's exported objects?
choices: interface compiles to smaller JavaScript
Consumers can augment the interface via declaration merging — adding fields without modifying your source
type aliases cause slower compilation
interface supports more TypeScript features
questions.explanation: Declaration merging lets library consumers add fields to your interface in their own code. `type` cannot be merged. This is how packages like Express extend `Request` with user-defined fields.

questions.prompt: Does this compile?
choices: Error — UserId is not string
Error — Status is not a valid function parameter type
Compiles — type aliases are transparent; 'abc' satisfies string, 'active' satisfies Status
Error — you cannot use literal unions as function parameters
questions.explanation: Type aliases are transparent — `UserId` IS `string`. `'abc'` is a valid string. `'active'` satisfies `'active' | 'inactive'`. No errors.

questions.prompt: When does TypeScript apply excess property checking on object literals?
choices: Always, on every object
Only when an object literal is passed directly to a typed location (parameter, variable with annotation)
Only for interface types, not type aliases
Only when the object has more than 5 properties
questions.explanation: Excess property checks are triggered only when TypeScript can attribute a fresh object literal to a specific type. Passing via an intermediate variable `const x = { ... }; fn(x)` bypasses the check.

questions.prompt: interface inheritance vs type intersection — what is the key difference?
choices: No difference — both achieve the same result
interface extends creates a subtype; type & creates an intersection that TypeScript treats differently in some edge cases (incompatible properties become never)
type & is always preferred because it's faster
interface extends is only for class hierarchies
questions.explanation: In most cases they're equivalent. However, if two intersected types have a property with incompatible types, `A & B` gives that property type `never` (the intersection of incompatibles). `interface extends` may give an error at declaration time. The IDE error messages also differ.

questions.prompt: What is the type of `x.name` here?
choices: string
number
string | number
never
questions.explanation: `string & number` is impossible — no value can be both. TypeScript resolves it to `never`. This is a signal that your intersection types are incompatible and the type can never be constructed.

questions.prompt: Which are true about structural typing in TypeScript?
choices: Two classes with the same shape are mutually assignable
A class instance can satisfy an interface if it has the required properties
TypeScript checks the declared type name at runtime
Extra properties make a value incompatible with a narrower type
questions.explanation: Structural typing: two classes with identical shapes are mutually assignable (unlike Java). Class instances satisfy interfaces structurally. TypeScript has zero runtime type knowledge. Extra properties make a value a SUBTYPE (more specific) — compatible with the narrower type, not incompatible.

questions.prompt: An optional property `name?: string` differs from `name: string | undefined` in that:
choices: No difference — TypeScript treats them identically
With ?, the property can be omitted entirely from the object. With | undefined, the key must be present but may be undefined.
? allows null; | undefined does not
| undefined is stricter and triggers more errors
questions.explanation: With `name?: string`, the key can be absent: `{}` is valid. With `name: string | undefined`, the key must be present: `{ name: undefined }` is required. This distinction matters for destructuring defaults and Object.keys() results.

questions.prompt: Which pattern lets you augment the global `Window` type with a custom property?
choices: type Window = { myProp: string }   // redeclare the type
interface Window { myProp: string }  // declaration merging
declare let window: { myProp: string }
You cannot augment built-in browser types
questions.explanation: Because `Window` is an `interface` in `lib.dom.d.ts`, you can merge into it: `interface Window { myProp: string }`. TypeScript merges your declaration with the existing one. This is why `interface` is preferred for extendable public API shapes.

flashcards.back: interface for object shapes you want to extend or merge. type for unions, tuples, mapped types, and everything else.
Two `interface X` declarations with the same name are merged. `type X` declared twice is an error. Use for library augmentation (e.g. adding fields to Express `Request`).
Compatibility = matching shape, not name. `{ x: number; y: number; extra: string }` satisfies `Point = { x: number; y: number }`. Extra fields are fine via variable; not via fresh literal.
Only on fresh object literals directly in a typed position. Pass via variable to bypass. Direct: error. Variable: passes if required fields present.
A literal-typed shared field (`kind`, `type`, `tag`) that TypeScript narrows on in switch/if. Each case gives you the correct variant with its unique fields unlocked.
`{ name: string } & { name: number }` gives `name: never` — `string & number` is impossible. A signal your type design has a conflict.
`readonly` on property = can't reassign the property. `const` on variable = can't reassign the reference. Neither prevents mutation of nested objects/arrays.
`prop?: T` — key can be absent. `prop: T | undefined` — key must be present, value may be undefined. Matters for destructuring, Object.keys(), and strictness settings.

project.brief: Design the type system for a notification service that handles three kinds of notifications: email (with `to`, `subject`, `body`), SMS (with `to`, `message`, `maxChars`), and push notification (with `deviceToken`, `title`, `badge`). 1) Model each as a member of a discriminated union — choose the tag field name and value. 2) Write a `send(n: Notification)` function signature that uses narrowing to handle each case. 3) Decide: should each variant be an `interface` or a `type` member? Why? 4) A third-party library augments your Notification type with an `analytics` field. Which approach lets consumers do this without modifying your source?

checklist.text: Discriminated union defined with a clear tag field and three variants
send() function uses narrowing (switch on tag) to access variant-specific fields
Justification given for interface vs type choice per variant
Explains why interface allows third-party augmentation via declaration merging

hints: Discriminated union tag: `type NotificationType = 'email' | 'sms' | 'push'`. Each variant has `kind: 'email'` etc.
Members of a union can each be `interface` — then consumers can declaration-merge extra fields onto each.
The `send` function switch exhausts all cases — add a `never` check on the default branch.
Third-party augmentation example: `interface EmailNotification { analytics: TrackingData }` — merges into your exported interface.
